data.title: India Gold Market Update: Volatility Softens Demand
data.description:
Prices ease, underlying strength prevails

Gold prices have pulled back from end-January highs, pressured by a stronger US dollar and a rise in US Treasury yields. In addition, outflows from gold ETFs, particularly US-listed gold ETFs, – likely indicating tactical profit taking from the sharp rally in January – further weighed on prices. However, the pullback was contained, as heightened geopolitical tensions and ongoing policy uncertainty continued to reinforce gold’s role as a safe-haven asset. Moreover, Asian demand has been strong, with trading during Asian hours contributing positively to returns (1.5% v/s -4.7% during US trading hours).1 Also, barring the US, ETF demand has been positive across other regions, and the COMEX net long positioning has continued to build.

Chart 1: Prices ease, levels stay elevated

Domestic gold prices diverged from international trends during February, declining 3.5%, even as international prices gained 5%.2 This divergence was primarily driven by an appreciation in the Indian rupee against the US dollar and fewer revisions in the customs tariff value.3 So far in March, domestic prices have realigned with international price trends: both moderating from end-February levels. However, as of 16 March, the decline in domestic prices (2.6%) has been less pronounced than the 4.4% decline in international prices, as the recent INR depreciation4 has cushioned the downside in local prices. Despite the recent pullback, gold prices remain firm on a y-t-d basis, with international prices up 14% and domestic prices rising 16% to INR 154,395/10g,5 underscoring that the broader uptrend remains in place.

Chart 2: Discounts persist

The domestic market has traded at a discount to international benchmarks since the second week of February. The discount briefly narrowed to par/slight-premium levels in early March on fears of supply tightness following geopolitical tensions involving Iran–US–Israel; disrupted flight routes from the UAE have interrupted India’s key supply channel, through which 24% of India’s gold was imported in 2025.

The move, however, was short-lived, with domestic prices reverting to a discount. Muted jewellery demand, along with likely earlier inventory build-up, ensured adequate supply. Discounts averaged US$58/oz during the first half of March, suggesting that near-term supply remains adequate relative to demand.

Volatility dents demand, structural support intact

Insights gathered from local market participants suggest that consumer demand for gold remained subdued despite the recent pullback from record highs. Buying interest was stronger during the earlier price uptrend – particularly on the investment side (bars and coins) – but has since softened amid heightened price volatility, leading consumers to defer purchases.

Seasonal and financial factors further weighed on demand. March is typically a softer month, marked by financial year-end closures and related statutory payments and tax outflows, which constrain liquidity for both consumers and retailers. Some retailers have reportedly liquidated inventory to meet tax obligations.

But the underlying trends remain constructive. Affluent consumers, who are relatively less sensitive to price levels, continue to support jewellery demand. Retailers are also reporting emerging interest from younger buyers, particularly for lightweight, contemporary, and predominantly plain gold jewellery (of 22k and 18k purity), which is increasingly viewed as an investment. Importantly, there is only limited evidence of profit-booking-driven liquidation, suggesting that sentiment towards gold remains positive, even as consumers gradually adjust to a higher price base. Retailer purchases, meanwhile, have been largely linked to store expansion and inventory requirements, rather than demand-led restocking.

Gold ETFs: inflow momentum continues alongside profit taking

Gold ETF inflows remained positive in February for the tenth consecutive month, albeit at a slower pace compared to the exceptionally strong inflows in the preceding two months. In line with our initial estimates, net inflows stood at INR52.5bn (US$579mn), translating into an addition of 3.3t to ETF gold holdings. The 78% m/m decline in net inflows in February was largely driven by tactical profit taking amid softer gold prices, with redemption during the month reaching a record INR250bn (US$276mn) as per AMFI data.

Despite the moderation on a sequential basis, February inflows remained well above the May-November 2025 average of INR 36.7bn (US$417mn), indicating that investor interest in gold ETFs remains relatively sticky. The trend has continued into March, with estimated net inflows of INR42bn (US$456mn) during the period 1-12 March; this additional 2.6t takes total holdings to 116.5t.

Investor participation continued to broaden during February, with around 0.65mn new folios (accounts) added, taking the total number of accounts directly invested in gold ETFs to 12.1mn. This highlights the growing adoption of gold ETFs within investor portfolios.

Regulatory developments could further support the segment’s growth. In February, Securities and Exchange Board of India (SEBI) introduced new rules6 allowing actively managed equity mutual funds to invest part of their residual portfolio allocation (up to 35% of assets beyond core equity exposure) in gold and silver ETFs, giving fund managers greater flexibility in portfolio diversification. The move could potentially lead to incremental institutional flows into gold ETFs. Reflecting the segment’s expanding role within the mutual fund industry, gold ETFs accounted for around 2.3% of overall mutual fund assets in February, broadly stable m/m but significantly higher than 0.9% one year ago.

A new gold ETF is being launched in March, taking the total number of gold ETFs in India to 26.7 A full list of the gold ETFs we track worldwide is available on Goldhub.com.

Chart 3: Inflows moderate, momentum holds

Sequential drop in imports

Gold imports moderated in February from the previous month, reflecting near-term demand softness amid elevated prices and volatility. However, imports remained significantly higher on an annual basis, rising 224% in value terms and over 80% in volume terms. At US$7.5bn, imports were 38% lower m/m but ~31% higher than the trailing 12-month average, indicating that underlying demand prevails despite likely price-led moderation in buying activity. In volume terms, February imports are estimated to be in the range of 48t–56t, notably lower than the 99t recorded in January but above the 31t of February 2025 and broadly in line with the 12-month average of 59t.

Late-February geopolitical developments in the Middle East are not yet reflected in the import data. Media reports8 indicate that flight disruptions have impacted gold shipments from key regional hubs, particularly the UAE. Any sustained disruption from the region could tighten near-term domestic supply conditions and prompt rerouting of imports.

Chart 4: Imports strong despite m/m dip

Footnotes

1Based on Asia, Europe and US trading window from 2 March to 18 March 2026 as estimated by World Gold Council.

2Based on MCX spot gold price in INR and LBMA Gold Price PM in USD.

3Customs duty on gold imports is levied on a notified value fixed by the Central Board of Indirect Taxes and Customs, rather than solely on invoice prices. There is no fixed schedule for revising the notified value. It is typically updated every few weeks and more frequently during periods when international price volatility is high.

4INR has depreciated against the USD by 1.6% during 1-16 March 2026.

5MCX spot gold price as of 16 March 2026.

6Categorization and Rationalization of Mutual Fund Schemes, SEBI, 26 February 2026.

7AMFI- New Fund Offer

8India staring at likely gold, diamond shortage after US and Israel strikes on Iran, Economic Times, 2 March 2026.

Original Post

data.title: Sunstone Hotel: Locking In Attractive Yields With Its Preferred Stock
data.description:
Introduction

Sunstone Hotel Investors (SHO) is a hotel REIT that’s running a pretty conservative balance sheet. The LTV ratio based on the book value of the assets is just around 30%, and likely even lower if you’d use the fair value of the hotel properties instead of the book value (which includes almost $1.5B in accumulated depreciation). I already have a long position in the REIT’s preferred shares, and am looking to add to this position to lock in a 7.5% yield.

A decent result ensures a strong preferred dividend coverage ratio

The final quarter of 2025 was better than I had expected for Sunstone Hotel Investors. As you can see below, the REIT reported a total FFO of $37.1M and an adjusted AFFO of $38.9M, mainly after adding back the amortization expense related to deferred stock compensation. This represents an AFFO/share of $0.20, and this brought the full-year AFFO per share to $0.86.

Keep in mind the FFO and AFFO already includes the impact from the preferred dividend payments. As the image above shows, Sunstone paid $16.1M in preferred dividends in 2025, and just under $4M in the final quarter of 2025. That reduction in quarterly preferred dividend payments is entirely related to the REIT buying back its own preferred shares. I’ll get back to that when I discuss Sunstone’s balance sheet.

Looking at the full-year results, the AFFO came in at $184M before taking the $16.1M in preferred dividends into consideration, which means the preferred dividends enjoy an excellent coverage ratio: Sunstone Hotel Investors needed to spend less than 10% of its AFFO and approximately 10% of its FFO on the preferred dividends.

A second element that I like to keep an eye on is the asset coverage ratio. As the balance sheet below shows, the book value of the real estate assets is approximately $2.77B and the total asset base of $3.03B is mainly funded by equity, as there are less than $1.1B in liabilities on the balance sheet.

Excluding the restricted cash, the $2.77B in book value of the assets only has approximately $809M in net financial debt against it, for an LTV ratio of approximately 30%. Also keep in mind the $2.77B book value already includes a $1.35B accumulated depreciation, so the fair value is likely substantially higher than the reported book value. This also means the LTV ratio based on the fair value would be even lower than the current 30%.

Sunstone Hotel Investors has also provided the outlook for 2026, and its management now expects to report an adjusted FFO of $153-178M. Using the lower end of that range, the preferred dividends still enjoy a 1,000% coverage ratio (and perhaps even slightly higher if Sunstone continues to buy back its own shares).

The first quarter of 2026 will be the strongest quarter of the year.

I still have a long position in both preferred shares

I have a long position in both series of preferred stock that are publicly trading, and I am quite indifferent between the two series: I just buy the preferred share with the highest yield as they both carry the same risk. The Series I preferred stock ( SHO.PR.I) offers a preferred dividend coupon of 5.7% (with four equal quarterly payments of just over $0.356 per quarter).

As the current share price of this security is just $19, the current yield is approximately 7.5% which I think is very attractive given the reasonably conservative balance sheet.

The main risks related to investing in the preferred shares are of course the potential for interest rates to spiral out of control, in which case the share price of the preferred stock will decrease. Additionally, a worsening economic situation and deteriorating consumer confidence level may result in a weaker-than-guided performance this year, and that’s why I used the lower end of the AFFO guidance as my base case scenario. But even if the AFFO would come in 25% below the low-end, the preferred dividends would still enjoy a coverage ratio of around 800%.

And now the REIT has addressed all debt maturities until 2028, the balance sheet is in a healthy position.

Investment thesis

I’m currently not interested in the common stock of Sunstone Hotel Investors but the preferred stock offers a risk/reward ratio I am definitely interested in. I already have a long position in both series of preferred shares, but I will likely add to my position in the coming days. I’m leaning towards the Series I, but I will just buy the series of preferred stock that makes the most sense when I’m ready to pull the trigger.

data.title: Sprout Social: Improving Fundamentals Pushing The Stock Into Deep Value Zone; Reiterating Buy
data.description:
In early December 2025, I published an article on Sprout Social (SPT) where I rated the stock a Buy. Since then, the stock price has gone south around 50%.

With this article, I want to discuss what has happened since then and what I expect for the future of the company. I maintain my Buy rating for the company because I think the market got it wrong with its opinion of the threat of AI to software-as-a-service (SaaS) companies and because I think Sprout Social can actually further improve revenue and profitability, thanks to AI.

What has changed since my last article

Q4 Earnings Report

We got the Q4 numbers, and they turned out the way I expected. In my last article, I wrote about how revenue growth is reaccelerating again with Q3 numbers. I wrote:

There is little reason to assume this is just a one-off quarter. It looks more like the beginning of a sustained turnaround.

And revenue growth reaccelerated in Q4, too. We can clearly see the downward trend has stopped since multiple quarters, and in the last two quarters, it is even reaccelerating.

However, the revenue guidance for 2026 was weak, with Q1 guided at $119.9 million-$120.7 million. At the midpoint of $120.3 million, this would result in a YoY growth of 10.1% (Q1 2025: $109.3 million). The full-year 2026 revenue was guided at $490.2 million-$495.2 million. At the midpoint of $492.7 million, this would result in a YoY growth of 7.7% (FY2025: $457.5 million).

The revenue growth graph would look like this for next year (in red):

But, we must note that Sprout did the same last year.

Weak Revenue Guidance History, a beat + raise story

With Q4 2024, the revenue guidance for 2025 was $443 million-$448 million.

In Q1 2025, this guidance was raised.

For the full year 2025, we're raising our guidance from the prior quarter and now expect revenue in the range of $448.9 million-$453.9 million.

In Q2 2025, the guidance was raised again.

For the full year 2025, we are raising our guidance from the prior quarter and now expect revenue in the range of $452.9 million-$455.9 million.

Also, with Q3 2025, the guidance was raised once again.

For the full year 2025, we are raising our guidance and now expect revenue in the range of $454.9 million-$455.7 million

And Q4 2025 shows the actual figure at $457.5 million, which was higher than the upwardly revised guidance.

I expect the same story to happen in 2026; here is why:

  • Number of customers with > $50,000 ARR grew by 18%
  • Average contract value grew by 16%

As I've already said in my previous article, Sprout Social has implemented a strategic shift where they now target large enterprise accounts. They also moved away from purely monthly subscriptions towards annual and multi-year contract deals. The numbers above from Q4 2025 show that the strategic shift is working.

  • Remaining performance obligations grew by 15%
  • Current Remaining Performance Obligations grew by 14%

Both numbers from Q4 2025 are much higher than the 7.7% yearly revenue growth guidance for 2026. If we look at cRPO alone, which basically is forward-looking revenue, it grew by 14% compared to last year. With a net retention rate of 100%, which Sprout has, it is difficult to believe that revenue would only grow 7.7% over the next 12 months.

  • Tailwind because of ChatGPT/Gemini/Claude and the decline in Google Search

Half of consumers use AI-powered search today, and it stands to impact $750 billion in revenue by 2028. Companies and brands now have to show up where their customers are to get discovered. Users now bounce between TikTok, Reddit, YouTube, ChatGPT, Gemini, and AI assistants before ever reaching a website.

Cloudflare CEO Matthew Prince recently presented data from the Cloudflare network, which, according to its statements, handles a significant portion of global internet traffic. Accordingly, the share of bot traffic has noticeably increased since the beginning of 2025. AI systems now provide answers directly instead of sending users to websites.

That's why social media is becoming more important for brands and companies to get discovered by customers, as an additional layer to back up the Google Search decline in recent months.

Profitability Plan

Sprout Social also announced a profitability plan, which is something I was asking for in my last article. This is huge, because in the past management has been telling us that it intends to continue investing in growth and does not expect to be profitable based on GAAP in the foreseeable future.

Let me reassure you on one point: Sprout Social has been generating positive cash flow since 2021. This is not about the company facing imminent bankruptcy risks of any kind. However, when revenue growth is declining, it is crucial to boost profitability. With the 30% target by Q4 2027, we can anticipate a combined revenue growth rate and profit margin exceeding 30%. This balance between growth and profitability enables sustainable scaling and enhances Sprout’s attractiveness to investors.

Special event to talk about the elephant in the room

Since my last article, we also got additional information about how Sprout Social is implementing AI into its software. This is an important topic, as software stocks have suffered in the last couple of months and have lost a lot of their market capitalization because of investors' fear that AI will destroy or replace SaaS companies.

Sprout Social recently had an event called "Platform Overview and System of Record and Action". You can find it here. Take a look at the VOD.

While valuation multiples for most software stocks got suppressed, I see AI as a great opportunity for Sprout Social. The roadmap with new features thanks to AI shows that Sprout Social is implementing AI for their customers - they are enabling AI and are not being replaced by AI.

Listening Agent

Listening Agent was presented at the Breaking Ground event in November 2025, and Sprout has received over 550 demo requests from customers, which was the biggest response ever. It now has over 1,000 beta customers, and a full release will likely be this month.

Insights Agent and Agent Studio

Insights Agent is in development (launch expected in the first half of 2026): The agent, for example, analyzes what’s been performing best, drafts new posts based on those patterns, and drafts the work so customers just have to review and approve the post to publish it.

Trellis Studio is in development (launch expected in the first half of 2026): the studio will give customers a library of pre-built tasks executed by the agents. Customers can configure them to run on a schedule or fire on a trigger. This is a platform play. As the library grows over time (new agents, tasks, features), the capabilities compound, and customers will be able to get increasing value.

AI-created boards

A future concept of an AI-created board: you describe what a user needs, and the agent will compose a board for that. The example here was built for a CEO who might want to engage with influencers when they interact with the brand. Instead of giving the CEO access to the full platform, you give them a board, the right data, AI-powered context, and the ability to take action all in one place.

The bigger idea here is that UIs no longer have to be hand-built for every single use case. Users will describe what they need, agents will build it, and the software will shape itself to each user.

Event Q&A Summary

I will add some questions and answers from the event because I think some of the answers add color to some pain points investors currently have.

Pain Point #1: Fewer seats/subscriptions are needed because AI will replace the workforce, so Sprout will lose revenue.

Question: What is your AI monetization philosophy, and how do you see the business model evolving over time?

Answer:

For Background agents that are doing more and more work for our customers, we'll be leaning into usage-based pricing, or kind of like outcome-based pricing and things like that.

My take: While I can understand the fear of fewer subscriptions, I don't see this as a long-term problem. Because it is easy to change the pricing model. Adobe (ADBE), for example, did it in 2011-2013, where they changed from a one-time payment model to a subscription-based model, and it became a successful business model innovation resulting in even more revenue. I don't see a reason why software companies could not change their pricing model again, this time to a usage-based or outcome-based model.

Pain Point #2: The software itself will be replaced by AI agents.

Question: On the topic of preferred access with the networks, what type of social data access does this give you versus what a generic AI agent would be able to ingest?

Answer:

It might be increased rate limits, it might be access to APIs before they're built. Several of the networks have come to us to ask our advice for how to build real-time streaming networks, because we're one of the biggest consumers of all these networks on the planet. And so, with most of the networks, we're actually working with them ahead of time to build these APIs to leverage for our customers. We get usually, like, much more elevated access to PII (Personally Identifiable Information), because we just do the right thing with that data, and don't share it when we don't need it, don't train when we shouldn't be training.

My take: Investors should remember we’re dealing with social data where privacy matters and numerous privacy laws apply. Therefore, I believe these large social networks won’t grant generic AI agents the same level of access as Sprout Social.

My summary around AI

We see a lot of development and new features, all thanks to AI. While the stock is currently valued at a P/S of 0.7x, because of AI, I think AI will actually help Sprout to make more revenue in the future, not less.

Sprout Social is used by small teams within companies and brands. They were never dependent on a significant amount of seat numbers, unlike other software companies. So the possible risk of fewer subscriptions or seats because of AI, in my opinion, does not affect Sprout Social as much compared to other SaaS companies, which have been around for a longer time.

Additional topics worth mentioning

Management SBC

I will take a close look at the next proxy and what management SBC will look like. Last quarter's earnings results show a clear picture, with net loss at $10.74 million and SBC expenses at $19.73 million. Just by reducing SBC, Sprout Social would be profitable based on GAAP. I am of the view that management's stock-based compensation has to be reduced when the stock price declines 50% in three months and by almost 80% in a year.

For software companies, SBC is not unusual. To compare the expenses between different companies, I like to use a stock-based compensation-to-revenue ratio. HubSpot, for example, had SBC at 15.6% of revenue last year. ServiceNow was at 13.8% last year, and Salesforce at 9.7%. None of those stocks lost 80% market cap in the last 12 months. While Sprout Social's SBC expenses are already declining (22.6% in Q3 2024), that figure at 16.3% of revenue is still higher compared to other software companies, so I hope management will look at it closely to gain interest from investors again.

According to the latest annual report, management has carried out two restructurings. One restructuring at the end of 2024 reduced headcount in Research & Development by roughly 10%. A second restructuring at the beginning of 2025 reduced headcount in Sales & Marketing. Taking both restructurings together, these alone resulted in SBC savings of $9.2 million in 2025 compared to the prior year.

However, instead of using these savings to improve GAAP profitability or to reduce dilution for existing shareholders, half of those savings went towards the management team as SBC expenses for the executive team increased by $4.8 million compared to the prior year. Now, after the stock price decline and the newly announced profitability plan, I expect management to reduce SBC for the executive team, and I'm looking forward to Sprouts Proxy Statement (expected at the end of April) for the 2026 Annual Meeting of Shareholders. After the CEO, Ryan Barretto, bought shares worth $1 million and co-founder Aaron Rankin bought shares worth $1 million, a reduction in SBC for executives could send another powerful signal that management will focus more on per-share value creation for shareholders in the future.

Dual-Class Structure

In December 2026, Sprout Social will transition from its dual-class structure. Class B shares, which currently have ten times the voting power of Class A shares, will be converted into Class A shares. This change could increase interest from potential acquirers or activist investors, as fewer shares are required to achieve sufficient voting power.

Free Cash Flow

Free cash flow increased from $4.4 million to $24.4 million (or, up 454%) between 2023 and 2024. It increased from $24.4 million to $39.3 million (up 61%) between 2024 and 2025. With a discounted cash flow valuation and a P/FCF target of 15x, the current fair value stock price would be around $11.

For my calculation, I have used the following assumptions (past averages do not include improved numbers after the new announced profitability plan):

  • Current Cash Flow Growth: 29.71% (Sprout's avg. in the past 5 years)
  • Current Free Cash Flow: $39.32 million
  • Current Diluted Shares Outstanding: 59.56 million
  • Yearly Growth Decay Rate: 35% (initial 29.71% becomes 19.3% the following year and so on)
  • Rate of Return: 10%
  • Annual Change to Share Count: +2.19%

Risks

Management could fail with their profitability plan and revenue growth, and/or margins could get worse; in this case, we could see the stock price under severe pressure.

Dilution through SBC on the executive level could persist, and it could take more time to reach GAAP profitability than it actually should. Shareholders and investors could abandon this stock, as they did in the last couple of months, which could lead to a further decline in stock price.

The recent positive results in Q3 and Q4 2025 with reaccelerating revenue growth could change course—back to slower revenue growth—in future quarters.

Conclusion

Sprout Social's stock price declined 50% after my initial coverage in December 2025. I thought the company was a bargain at a P/S of 1.4x then. Now, after Q4 earnings, the AI event, and the new profitability plan, and at an attractive P/S multiple, it became an even better deal. Therefore, I will maintain my Buy rating as the fundamentals have improved, in my view.

Could it be a value trap? Maybe. But to me, at the current valuation, the market already expects the company to go bankrupt. I view this as unlikely given the P/S of 0.7x, even as revenue is growing, margins and profitability are improving, and free cash flow is growing.

It can take some time for this stock to recover, as it looks like short sellers (short interest has increased above 10%) currently dictate the price. But when this stock recovers, it will recover massively, in my opinion.

data.title: Jobs Data, PMI In Focus With A Muted Earnings Calendar Next Week
data.description:
Get ahead of the market by subscribing to Seeking Alpha's Wall Street Week Ahead, a preview of key events scheduled for the coming week. The newsletter keeps you informed of the biggest stories set to make headlines, including upcoming IPOs, investor days, earnings reports, and conference presentations.

Wall Street's major averages were in the red on Friday, with oil sustaining higher prices even as Israeli Prime Minister Netanyahu said the country is helping to reopen the Strait of Hormuz. Oil prices slipped late on Thursday on the news that Benjamin Netanyahu said Iran no longer has the capacity to enrich uranium or make ballistic missiles. Meanwhile, the U.S. military is reportedly sending thousands more Marines to the Middle East, including three warships, according to U.S. officials.

With no major earnings scheduled, investors will keep a keen eye on key economic data releases next week. S&P Global PMI data for March is due on Tuesday. Crude oil inventory data will be out on Wednesday. On Thursday, initial jobless claims data will be released.

GameStop (GME), BYD (BYDDF), and Carnival Corp. (CCL) are among the companies reporting their results next week.

_______________________________________________________________

Earnings spotlight: Tuesday: GameStop. See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Wednesday: PDD (PDD), Beyond Meat (BYND). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Thursday: Pony Ai (PONY). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Friday: BYD, Carnival Corp. See the full earnings calendar.

Volatility watch: Coinbase (COIN) and lululemon (LULU) have a high level of implied volatility based on options trading. Sunrun (RUN) and Omega Therapeutics (OMGAQ) head into the new week with a high level of short interest outstanding on them. The most overbought stocks per their 14-day relative strength index include Tilly's (TLYS), Adecoagro (AGRO), and Kore Group (KORE). The most oversold stocks per their 14-day Relative Strength Index include Crane Harbor Acquisition (CHAC), Lifecore Biomedical (LFCR), and HNI (HNI).

IPO watch: No new IPOs are expected to price during the week and begin trading. The IPO lockup period expires for certain blocks of shares of Megan Holdings (MGN). The analysts' quiet period ends on Generate Biomedicines (GENB) to free up analysts to post ratings.

Dividend watch: Companies that have an ex-dividend date coming next week include Broadcom (AVGO), Altria (MO), Seagate (STX), and DICK'S Sporting Goods (DKS).

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Strive Asset Management: Sustained Dip Reflects Flawed Bitcoin As A Treasury Push
data.description:
Strive Asset Management (ASST) has seen a previously unhinged premium to its Bitcoin (BTC-USD) holdings erode over the last 6 months, with the stock down 88%. This has underperformed by more than 2x the 40% dip in BTC over the same time period. The security and its preferreds offer an easy way for retail investors to add BTC exposure directly to their investment accounts. Bears flag that it's difficult to build a bullish case beyond this for ASST versus buying BTC directly. ASST currently holds 13,628 BTC, with the cryptocurrency currently trading for $71,091 per BTC, to place the total value of this position at $968 million. ASST had 69,158,785 basic average common shares outstanding as of the end of its fiscal 2025 fourth quarter, for a market cap of around $700 million as it is currently trading hands for $10.26 per share. I last covered the ticker with a Hold rating.

Critically, ASST bought its BTC at a markedly average higher price of $104,367 per BTC, which means it is underwater by around $454 million on its holdings. This is a loss of around 32%. This difference between its average price and current market price reflected across its total BTC reserve is shareholder value lost on a business model that I think will find it hard to replicate the success championed by Microstrategy (MSTR). This hinged on the common shares of the BTC holder trading at a premium to its BTC reserve to provide a backdrop for the sustained issuance of new common shares to buy more BTC. ASST is currently trading at an enterprise value to BTC NAV ratio ("mNAV") of just 1.05x. For some context, this metric would have been well north of 10x against ASST's previous 52-week high. The current ratio is still above 1x, which means ASST can issue new common shares through its ongoing at-the-market ("ATM") capacity at a pace that optimises its BTC yield. To be clear, the company held total cash of $67.4 million as of the end of its fourth quarter. New BTC purchases have to be made with newly issued shares, with the company also tapping its Variable Rate Series A Perpetual Preferred Stock (SATA) as currency for BTC purchases.

Operational Layer, Financing Costs, And Risk

A key drawback of owning BTC via a treasury company centers on adding a layer of costs to cryptocurrency. ASST has between 11 and 50 employees according to its LinkedIn profile, with its recently filed Form 10-K providing a more granular figure of 28 full-time employees ("FTEs") as of the end of the fourth quarter. Critically, the company completed its all-stock acquisition of Semler Scientific post-period end. Semler was an early adopter of BTC as a reserve. The company is in the healthcare space, providing medical technology and software to enable point-of-care testing for early chronic disease detection. Semler has around 79 FTEs as per the last filed Form 10-K for its 2024. ASST's selling, general, and administrative expenses ("SG&A") came in at $5 million for its recent fourth quarter, which, in aggregate with Semler's SG&A expenses, convey the layer of cost above and beyond just holding BTC directly that holders will have to take on.

Buying ASST at this level strictly for its BTC exposure would be a belief that the company will proportionally outperform BTC. This was the prior bull case for MSTR, but I believe this zeitgeist has come to an end with the proliferation of BTC as a treasury companies. There is at least 65 publicly listed U.S. companies buying and holding BTC directly on their balance sheets. ASST also spent $4.3 million on dividend payments to SATA shareholders in the period from September 12, 2025 to December 31, 2025. This is set to rise with the closing of a January follow-on offering of 1,320,000 shares of SATA at a price of $90 per share. The combination of financing expenses, an operational layer, and a negative return on an average BTC buying price that's materially higher than the current range of the currency portends a fundamental erosion of the need to own ASST versus buying BTC directly. This is not to say BTC is a bad investment, but that owning BTC via ASST is setting up for a negative investment base. For SATA holders, ASST held a dividend reserve of 18 months as of March.

The BTC Price

Bulls would highlight that the malaise with the stock reflects BTC prices that have dropped, and a recovery of this still represents upside for the stock, especially with it already trading at such a narrow range. This means that any rally in BTC presents a significant risk factor for bears, with the scale of this risk magnified exponentially if this rally is overlayed with a recovery in ASST's mNAV. To be clear, ASST could see its stock price double even if BTC moves up less than this amount if its mNAV sees strong growth in addition to the price of BTC moving up.

Conclusion

ASST's BTC as a treasury model will find it difficult to replicate the historical success of MSTR, with a narrow premium as per mNAV set to constrain the overall cadence of capital market access via common shares, instead forcing the use of more expensive preferreds with SATA currently with a 12.75% coupon. I am rating ASST as a Sell.

data.title: Managed Futures ETF Guide: What It Is, How It Works, And Why KMLM
data.description:
Most investors are familiar with stocks and bonds. Fewer are familiar with managed futures, and yet, some of the world's largest institutional investors, including major pension funds, have been allocating to managed futures strategies for decades. Today, managed futures ETFs make this institutional-grade strategy accessible to a much broader range of investors. The KraneShares Mount Lucas Managed Futures Index Strategy ETF (KMLM) is one such vehicle, offering systematic exposure to global macro trends across commodities, currencies, and fixed income futures.

What Is a Managed Futures ETF?

A managed futures ETF is an exchange-traded fund that provides exposure to a portfolio of futures contracts, standardized agreements to buy or sell an asset at a future date and price, across a range of global markets. Unlike traditional ETFs that largely hold stocks or bonds, a managed futures ETF gains exposure to price trends in commodities (such as oil, gold, and agricultural products), global currencies, and fixed-income instruments, such as government bond futures.

The "managed" component refers to the systematic, rules-based methodology used to decide which futures to hold, and critically, whether to hold them long (benefiting from rising prices) or short (benefiting from falling prices). This two-directional flexibility is one of the most important features of managed futures strategies and something that traditional stock or bond ETFs often cannot offer.

Price trends or volatility refer to fluctuations in market prices that may create both gains and losses. While some managed futures strategies seek to respond to sustained price movements, periods of heightened volatility may also result in increased losses, particularly when price movements are short-lived or inconsistent.

How Does a Managed Futures ETF Work?

Managed futures strategies are typically built on trend-following, a systematic approach that identifies and follows price momentum across markets. When prices are trending upward, the strategy goes long. When prices are trending downward, it goes short. The logic is straightforward: trends persist because of real economic forces, and markets are slow to adjust.

Futures markets exist primarily so that commercial businesses, farmers, energy companies, and airlines can transfer price risk to investors willing to hold it. Managed futures strategies systematically accept this price risk and may earn a premium for doing so. This dynamic creates a return stream that is fundamentally different from equities or bonds because it is driven by price volatility and macro trends rather than corporate earnings or credit risk.

Macro trends, sustained price movements driven by broad economic shifts, policy changes, or geopolitical developments, are what managed futures strategies are designed to capture. These trends have historically emerged across commodity markets, currencies, and global interest rates, creating opportunities for systematic trend-following strategies to generate returns that are largely independent of whether stocks or bonds are rising or falling.

Key features of how a managed futures ETF works:

  • Long and short exposure: Can profit in both rising and falling markets
  • Diversified across asset classes: Commodities, currencies, and global fixed income*
  • Rules-based and systematic: Removes emotional decision-making from the investment process
  • Exchange-traded: Daily liquidity, transparent holdings, accessible through a standard brokerage account

Why Add a Managed Futures ETF to a Portfolio?

The traditional 60/40 portfolio, 60% stocks and 40% bonds, has long been considered a balanced approach. However, this framework has shown limitations during certain periods when stocks and bonds declined together, as they did in 2022, when inflation-related shocks contributed to both asset classes selling off simultaneously.1

This is where some investors may consider managed futures ETFs as part of a broader portfolio discussion. Because managed futures derive their returns from price trends and macro volatility, they have historically exhibited low or even negative correlation with stocks and bonds during certain market stress environments.2 However, correlations can vary materially over time and may not be consistent across all market conditions.

The potential portfolio benefits of a managed futures ETF include:

  • Diversification: Low historical correlation to stocks and bonds*2
  • Crisis alpha: Historically strong performance during equity drawdowns and macro dislocations2
  • Inflation sensitivity: Commodity and currency exposure can provide a natural hedge during inflationary environments
  • Positive skew: A return profile in which gains and losses may be unevenly distributed over time, with outcomes that can differ between quiet markets and periods of market stress

Some of the world's largest institutional investors have been rethinking traditional asset allocation frameworks in favor of a more holistic approach. The Total Portfolio Approach (TPA), embraced by pension giants like the California Public Employees' Retirement System (CalPERS) and the California State Teachers' Retirement System (CalSTRS), focuses on managing risk and return at the total portfolio level, creating a natural home for diversifying strategies like managed futures. Managed futures are not designed to outperform stocks in every environment. They are designed to complement stocks and bonds by performing differently across economic regimes.

What Is KMLM?

The KraneShares Mount Lucas Managed Futures Index Strategy ETF is a managed futures ETF that seeks to track the KFA MLM Index, a diversified trend-following portfolio of commodity, currency, and global fixed income futures contracts traded on U.S. and international exchanges.

KMLM provides exposure to three broad asset categories:

  • Commodities: Including energy, metals, and agricultural markets
  • Currencies: Major global foreign exchange pairs
  • Fixed Income: Government bond futures across major developed markets

The strategy is systematic and rules-based, meaning position sizes and directional exposures are determined by the index methodology rather than discretionary forecasts, with exposures adjusted based on predefined trend-following rules. This removes behavioral bias and ensures consistent application of the trend-following approach across all market environments.

Why KMLM Among Managed Futures ETFs?

Not all managed futures ETFs are constructed the same way. KMLM is differentiated by several characteristics that make it a compelling choice for investors seeking managed futures exposure:

  • Deep index history: The KFA MLM Index methodology has a track record dating back to January 1988, providing a long, rigorous historical dataset across multiple market regimes, including the Global Financial Crisis, the COVID crash, and the 2022 inflation shock.
  • Diversification across asset classes*: By spanning commodities, currencies, and fixed income futures across global markets, KMLM seeks exposure to price movements across multiple asset classes, rather than concentrating in a single asset class.
  • Positive skew: Unlike stocks and bonds, which historically have exhibited negative skew, managed futures strategies like KMLM have a return distribution characterized by uneven outcomes over time, which may differ across market environments, including quieter markets and periods of market stress.
  • ETF structure: KMLM delivers managed futures exposure through an exchange-traded fund, meaning daily liquidity, transparent holdings, and accessibility through a standard brokerage account, without the complexity of direct futures trading or the fee structures of traditional hedge funds.
  • No equity exposure: Many managed futures ETFs incorporate equity futures or equity-linked components in their portfolios. KMLM does not. This is an intentional design choice. Equities are typically the dominant risk factor in most investor portfolios, and adding equity exposure inside a managed futures allocation can increase correlation to stocks during periods of market stress, precisely when diversification is needed most*. By limiting exposure to only commodities, currencies, and fixed-income futures, KMLM seeks to provide exposure to asset classes that differ from a strategy that behaves like the rest of the portfolio when conditions deteriorate.

How to Think About Managed Futures Performance

One of the common mistakes investors make is evaluating a managed futures ETF the same way they evaluate stocks. Because managed futures strategies are designed to perform differently from equities, not to beat them in all environments, comparing them on a simple side-by-side return table may not provide the full picture.

The right question is not "did KMLM beat the S&P 500 this year?" The right question is "did KMLM fulfill its role in the total portfolio?" A managed futures allocation is more analogous to insurance than to a growth investment, as they are typically discussed in terms of their potential portfolio role rather than return maximization.

Investors who maintain a disciplined allocation to managed futures during quiet periods can be positioned to benefit when macro volatility returns. Trimming the allocation during stretches of underperformance risks removing exactly what the strategy is designed to provide.

Conclusion

A managed futures ETF like KMLM offers investors something genuinely different from the stocks and bonds that make up most portfolios: systematic exposure to global macro trends, the ability to go both long and short, and a historical track record of performing well in environments that challenge traditional allocations most.

As geopolitical uncertainty, shifting central bank policy, and macro volatility continue to shape the investment landscape, the case for including a managed futures ETF as a diversifying allocation in a broader portfolio remains compelling.* KMLM provides a transparent, liquid, and institutionally grounded way to access this strategy.

References to institutional investors are provided solely for market context and educational purposes and do not imply endorsement, validation, or suitability of any investment strategy for retail investors.

*Diversification does not ensure a profit or guarantee against a loss. KMLM is non-diversified. The statement regarding diversification refers to sector exposure, not regulatory diversification under the Investment Company Act of 1940.

Citation:

  1. Data from Bloomberg as of 3/13/26.
  2. Data from Bloomberg and Mount Lucas as of 12/31/2022, retrieved on 12/31/2025.

Definitions:

  1. KFA MLM Index ("KMLM's Index"): The KFA MLM Index is a diversified trend-following portfolio of commodity, currency, and global fixed income futures contracts traded on U.S. and foreign exchanges. The performance data for the index is a representation of the MLM Index from 1/1/1988 to 12/31/2004, the MLM Index EV ("EV"), with enhanced Execution and Volatility characteristics, from 1/1/2005 to 11/30/2020, and, using the same methodology as the "EV," the KFA MLM Index from 12/1/2020 on.
  2. S&P 500 Index: A market capitalization-weighted index tracking the performance of 500 leading publicly traded companies in the United States, broadly representing the large-cap equity market. Inception date: March 4, 1957.
  3. Bloomberg US Aggregate Bond Index ("The Agg"): A broad-based, market capitalization-weighted bond market index representing intermediate-term investment-grade bonds traded in the United States. Inception date: January 1, 1976.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: VFMF ETF: This Multi-Factor Value ETF Meets The Moment
data.description:
VFMF - Finally Proving Its Worth

The Vanguard U.S. Multifactor ETF Shares (BATS:VFMF) is an actively managed exchange-traded fund (also known as an ETF) that invests in U.S. stocks using a rules-based quantitative model to evaluate U.S. common stocks and construct an equity portfolio that looks at multiple factors.

The point of this ETF is to have an actively managed option to track value stocks within the U.S. market, using a factor-based approach, with the fund manager rebalancing and reviewing the fund often for investment opportunities. This fund is a multifactor ETF that specifically targets stocks that the advisor thinks have strong recent performance, strong fundamentals, and low prices relative to fundamentals.

Unlike general value ETFs like the Vanguard Value Index Fund ETF Shares (VTV), which simply include all stocks classified as "value" within an index, VFMF employs a factor-based selection process to rank and weight stocks based on their value characteristics. The fund builds its portfolio using three main factors: value, momentum, and quality. The value is assessed using price-to-book, forward price-to-earnings, and operating price-to-cash flow ratios. Meanwhile, momentum is evaluated through three metrics: total returns from the last 12 months and 6 months and the intercept from a one-year regression of returns against their regional benchmark.

The quality factor varies by sector: for financials, it includes return on equity and share issuance, while for non-financials, it incorporates return on equity, gross profitability, changes in net operating assets, and leverage. VFMF also removes 20% of the most volatile stocks within each market capitalization group for risk management. All that to say, there's a clear mandate of what the fund is trying to do from a broad index perspective.

VFMF's benchmark target is the Russell 3000 Index, and this article will tell you whether it's worth investing with VFMF or another passive asset manager/fund when thinking about investing in value-focused mid-to-large cap equities. Passive investing in ETFs means that the fund managers don't make many trades versus active managers, who have the mandate to buy and sell stocks more often if they feel that there's a compelling investment opportunity - though often at higher fees.

I've been sour on VFMF in the past. My last article, written in the fall, highlighted how it had underperformed its benchmark and SPY significantly, and in Q3, it continued to underperform. But since then, the fund has held up in the face of global geopolitical adversity. It's over performed as value has remained stable during the war, while energy has surged - and that sector fits well within the VFMF multi-factor framework alongside financials and industrials. I'm upgrading this name to a Buy - as even though its only up about 1% year to date, the sectors its positioned in should keep this ETF afloat as the market stabilizes.

Fund Breakdown

VFMF was launched on Feb. 13, 2018, by Vanguard with a mandate to evaluate all the securities in an investment universe comprised of U.S. stocks and to build an equity portfolio that looks to achieve exposure to multiple factors using a rules-based screen.

The idea behind the fund is to promote diversification and to mitigate exposure to certain less liquid and more volatile stocks. The mandate makes sense, but I'm sometimes a bit dubious of these passively managed funds that try to find "growth" or "value" within these larger distinct buckets (i.e., small-cap, mid-cap, etc.). If you look at the returns on some of these funds, VFMF included, there isn't always a link to see the risk vs. reward returns that are mentioned in the prospectus strategy as the target. That said, the fund reasonably provides its mandate, has been pretty stable during the war, and the portfolio has had a 50% turnover, which is in line with many actively managed funds.

That aside, the fund has been trading for years, with a NAV of $559MM and a 50-day daily volume of almost 23,000 shares traded worth ~$3.5MM. Over the past few months, even before the war started, a broadening in the market was taking place - as seen below, AI had driven multiples too high and value was winning to start 2026. And amidst geopolitical uncertainty, I prefer having exposure to stocks around 10-15x earnings with stable cash flows vs. AI hype as oil fields get bombed in the middle east. Even with the drop since late February, the fund is about 5% above the Russell 3000.

The fund also employs a 0.18% yearly ETF expense ratio for fees to the asset manager, similar and negligible to IWV.

Fund Portfolio & Rebalancing Profile

When comparing VFMF's portfolio to peers, there are some differences in the portfolio breakdown that skew value and quality. As of the end of 2025, the portfolio holds stocks with an average P/E of 16.2 and P/B of 2, which clearly notes that they are excluding higher growth stocks - and even their website has not yet rebalanced. By the end of March, I expect energy to have been about 10% of the portfolio alongside increased weights for health care and industrials.

Additionally, the fund has been weighted more significantly in small caps versus the Russell 3000 Index while also holding significantly more financials and consumer discretionary, while reducing its tech holdings, giving investors a balance across cap sizes.

From a rebalancing perspective, right now, this kind of framework actually makes sense. With geopolitical noise running hot, commodity prices swinging wildly, and rate expectations shifting every other week, having a model that naturally tilts toward companies with stronger balance sheets, more resilient cash flows, and relative price strength is an advantage. And the fact that the methodology explicitly strips out the most volatile names within each market cap bucket is a built-in guardrail — providing some downside protection without the fund manager needing to make a big macro call.

The other thing I like is that the portfolio isn't static. When commodity momentum picks up, the model incrementally adds energy and materials exposure; when a business starts deteriorating, it gets trimmed. That factor rebalancing is what separates a solid factor fund from a fund that just buys cheap-looking stocks, and for now, this framework makes sense given the instability in the market.

Risks

From a risk perspective, a beta of 1 for VFMF notes that the fund has the same volatility as the benchmark. This is a bit surprising given that value stocks should be a bit less volatile over time, but with the fund's recent outperformance and additional geopolitical volatility, this should adjust over time toward 0.8-0.9. Additionally, when evaluating the fund's risk-adjusted returns, I analyzed the Sharpe ratio to see whether the fund has generated excess returns over the risk-free rate when adjusted for total volatility. The Sharpe ratio for the fund is 0.88, below the score of 1+ to prove strong risk-adjusted returns, but not too bad.

From a macroeconomic perspective, small-cap stocks are more sensitive to interest rate fluctuations. I agree with investors' sentiment that value is a better way to have equity exposure than growth, but it's still worth cautioning.

Bottom Line

The bottom line for this fund, in my opinion, is that while a historical underperformer, it's right for the moment. The multi-factor weights in value and quality have stabilized during a chaotic month of March. Given the current economy, I think it's worth a cautious Buy. If the war ends quickly and oil prices fall, which I doubt, then it may eventually slide back to an underperformer.

data.title: How I Replaced My Salary With Dividend Income From My Portfolio
data.description:
Co-authored with Beyond Saving

While some are born into a life of luxury where they never have to worry about paying a bill, most of us weren't. I remember when I was 20-something, looking out at the world with all sorts of dreams and not really knowing what those dreams meant. I thought I wanted to be a lawyer! Then I interned at a law firm and saw that what lawyers actually do is absolutely nothing like Perry Mason. That dream went to the wayside when I learned that the reality of the dream wasn't something that I was really passionate about.

I didn't have a chance to find what I really loved to do. Life happened, bills had to be paid, and I didn't have the luxury of finding my "dream job.". Nobody was going to support me while I tested out a dozen fields to find what I liked. A job was just something that I was good at and brought in enough money to pay the bills.

If I'm being honest, I was a bit jealous of those who went straight into a career that they absolutely loved. For me, a job was a cubicle, spreadsheets, and presentations because it's what I was good at, and the salary was good.

I'm sure many of you are in the same situation. Waking up in the morning, driving through rush hour, to sit at a desk doing a job that you might not hate, but you don't love. Maybe you are like I was, dreaming about being able to get up every morning and doing something that you love doing so much you'd do it for free.

Today, I'm blessed to be able to wake up every morning and do something that I truly love. I no longer dread the end of a vacation. I wake up, and I'm truly excited to dive into my work.

I'm here to tell you two things.

  1. There's no replacement for loving the work you do every day. Sure, there are always things in any career that aren't fun, but when you truly have a passion for the work you do. The grass is, in fact, greener when you find that one thing that you truly love to do.
  2. It's possible to change your life and escape the rat race.

I know these things because I was there. I was in a job of necessity that failed to excite me. It isn't easy. The path of least resistance is to just suck it up, work until you can retire, and start living the life you want when you're 65.

Yet for those who are willing to take the rougher path, you can set yourself up financially to take the risk, explore other options, and find your happy place in this world.

Preparing to Lose an Income

Why do people do jobs they don't love? Because they need money. And they don't need to go win a lump sum playing the lottery. They need a recurring source of income that they can use every month to pay the bills.

Odds are that you can't even explore your options because you're working 40-60 hours per week. You might have family obligations, you might have debt, and you don't have a lot of time or energy to devote to something new. You need something to replace the income you currently make from working.

When I made the decision that I wanted to leave my cubicle, I set a 10-year goal. Within 10 years, I wanted to have the freedom to walk away from my job. My approach was to attack the problem from two angles – expenses and income.

Control the Growth of Your Expenses

As you progress in any job, you will find your income rising. Our natural inclination whenever we get a promotion and raise is to increase our spending. It is known as "lifestyle creep," where as people earn more, they get used to a higher standard of living. You purchase a bigger house, and not only do you have a higher mortgage, but you also have higher maintenance, electricity, insurance, property taxes, etc. Maybe you start buying more expensive food, driving a more expensive car, splurging more on vacation, or wearing more expensive clothes. So while you make more money, you don't actually have more cash because your expenses increase to match it.

When I decided I was serious about early retirement, I realized that I had to cut back my expenses. When I got raises, I didn't increase my standard of living. While my peers were getting larger houses and driving fancier cars, I lived in the same place and drove the same old car.

It makes a huge difference. Consider a car loan. Say you take out a $25,000 car loan at 5% interest. How much does that cost you? Well, we can look at an amortization schedule and add it up:

We paid $25,000 in principal, and paid $3,306.85 in interest. Not bad, right? But flip it around. What if instead of paying $470/month on a car loan, we put it in investments with an average yield of 8%?

We "pay" the same $28,306.85 out of our paycheck, and those investments earn us dividends of $4,906.15. So, by choosing to take out a car loan, we pay $3,306.85 in interest, while also missing out on $4,906.15 in dividends. This isn't even counting the money spent on higher insurance and other expenses that are higher for a newer car than an older one.

It's the same effect for any debt.

There are two types of people in this world:

  1. People who pay interest.
  2. People who collect interest.

Be someone who collects interest.

Maximize Your Income

It really isn't complicated. The more money you're plowing into your investment account, the faster it will grow and the sooner you can achieve your goals.

Most of us are either making money or spending money. My cubicle job was salary, and I usually worked about 45 hours a week. That left a lot of time to be filled, where I didn't want to be spending money. So, I found ways to make money.

I was fortunate in that while looking for ways to make money, I also discovered my passion. The first time I was published, I made a grand total of $46.61. Yeah, that wasn't going to replace my job. But I enjoyed it. It took time, it stimulated me intellectually, and instead of paying to have fun, I was being paid to have fun. Over a weekend where I would have spent $50 "going out" and doing whatever, I made $50. No, it wasn't a lot. And at the time, I didn't imagine that I would ever make enough money writing to replace my day job. Its value was primarily that it occupied my time and prevented me from the temptation to spend more.

The growth of the Internet has accelerated the "gig economy," and that's a huge advantage for people looking to make small amounts of money with very flexible time.

Example Budget Savings

Here are the things that I saved money on, that most of my peers at a similar income level had:

  • Instead of paying $1,400/month on a mortgage, I paid $700/month rent.
  • Instead of $100/month for cable TV, I had an antenna.
  • Instead of $400/month for a car payment and hundreds more for comprehensive insurance, I drove old cars with liability insurance and walked to work.
  • Instead of eating out, I cooked at home.

These changes saved +$1,500/month, which was plowed directly into my brokerage account. In addition to the money that all my other peers were saving.

Real-World Results

Of course, in the real-world, your investments aren't going to be consistent. Prices change, dividends get cut, and there will be fluctuations both in the value and income your portfolio is producing. However, when you are aggressively adding, these fluctuations are to your benefit.

Consider a simplified portfolio that includes:

PIMCO Corporate & Income Opportunity Fund (PTY)

Royce Small-Cap Trust Inc (RVT)

Liberty All-Star Equity Fund (USA)

A debt fund, a small-cap fund, and a large-cap fund.

Here's how the income would have grown for the 15 years from 2003 (inception of PTY) through 2017, investing a flat $2,500/month:

Note the decline in 2009 because of dividend cuts; it resulted in a huge rebound from 2010-2013 because the dividends recovered, and since this portfolio kept buying, many more shares were owned. Low prices benefit buyers. In year 11 (2013), this portfolio produced over $56k in dividends.

If this same investment started in 2015, then by year 11 (2025), it would have produced slightly less, about $55k in dividends:

Note that the largest increases in income occurred following COVID and during the recovery from the 2022 dip.

There are no guarantees, and different periods will lead to different results. It's hard to predict 10-years out. However, if you're consistently investing, your portfolio will grow, and if you're consistently investing in income stocks, your income will grow.

Was It Worth It?

I started with a 10-year plan with the goal of replacing roughly half of my income. I knew I had to be aggressive, and I knew I had to sacrifice. I'd be lying if I said that I was never tempted to dip into my brokerage account to make life easier and more comfortable.

I avoided the temptation, and as a result, was able to leave my job after just seven years and take that dive into the unknown. But, it wasn't so scary because I knew that my portfolio was producing enough income to meet my essential needs and that any job on top of it was enough to provide a more comfortable lifestyle than I had been living voluntarily.

Today, I get to do what I love. "Work" isn't something that I dread or look forward to ending. It's something that I look forward to, that I'm passionate about, and that makes me feel good. To me, that's priceless.

I can't tell you what is right for you. What I can tell you is that if you want to set yourself up to leave your current career early, it isn't an easy road. It's a 4x4 trail full of challenges. Replacing your income with interest and dividends isn't easy. It means that you can't be blowing all your income on things that you want right now. It means your peers, who are making about the same salary, will be living visibly more comfortable lives. It isn't a sacrifice everyone is willing to make.

For me, it was the best decision of my life. It's frustrating when early on that income stream is tiny, it's the most rewarding thing in the world to watch it compound and become a torrent of cash, and you realize you make more money outside of work than you do putting in your 8-5.

Dividend investing changed my life. Today, I get the joy of sharing my knowledge to change the lives of others.

data.title: NetApp: Robust Profitability And AI Inference Tailwind Poised To Propel This Stock To New Heights
data.description:
Q3 2026 Earnings Takeaway

For Q3 2026, NetApp, Inc.(NTAP) reported a revenue and EPS of $1.71 billion and $2.12, respectively, which beat the analyst consensus of $1.69 billion and $2.07, respectively.

Turning over to key segmental quarterly results, Hybrid Cloud, which represents NetApp's storage products, support and services, delivered a revenue of $1.54 billion, up 5% YoY, while Public Cloud's revenue was $174 million, flat YoY. However, excluding the divested Spot revenue, NetApp Public Cloud's revenue grew by 17% YoY.

NetApp's consolidated gross margin was 71.2%, up 50 basis points(0.50%) YoY, driven by Public Cloud and Support services' outstanding gross margins of 85.1% and 92.5%, respectively. The attractiveness of these two divisions is their stickiness and criticality among enterprise customers.

First, in Public Cloud, this segment is a beneficiary of the enterprise's continued preference for both on-premises and cloud storage solutions or 'hybrid.' A hybrid storage implementation enables an enterprise to meet its required performance, data confidentiality and regulatory compliance requirements. NetApp's Public Cloud provides a seamless bridge between their bread and butter enterprise on-premise storage and the cloud, enabling them to keep habituating customers to their software platform. Switching cost is also at play here. Rather than finding other solutions providers to provide the bridge, enterprise IT Decision Makers(ITDM) often choose to stay with NetApp to avoid the costly learning curve, potential failure of an uncertain new provider, and switching costs, given NetApp's economies of scale. Additionally, NetApp's public cloud enables it to participate in the growth of Cloud Computing TAM, which, according to Gartner, will grow at a CAGR of 20% until 2028. While I'm always skeptical of the 'projections' or 'forecasting,' it doesn't hurt the company to participate in 'potentially' high growth segments or markets especially if it's a natural extension of the business's core.

On Support, with how delicate and crucial enterprise data is, enterprises are willing to pay a premium for a 'peace of mind.' This explains the business's absurd gross margin. Reliability on NetApp's core product also plays an important role in this business's gross margin as enterprise customers won't risk going with suppliers with a poor track record for their crucial data. Imagine having a business where you sell a product so reliable that alone it guarantee's 98% of its deliverables, and customers are still willing to pay extra for assurance that it will continue to do so. This enables NetApp to allocate just enough resources to deliver if that 2% happens. This is akin to the classic Razor and Blade business model, where NetApp's razor is its storage products, while the blade is the high-margin support that provides the peace of mind. Likewise, NetApp's typical gross margin for the quarter is 55.3% compared to NetApp Support's 92.5%. For the long-term, NetApp management targets a gross margin for products in the mid-to-high 50%.

Hybrid Cloud's gross margin, which includes NetApp's products, services, and support, is at 69.6%, down 1.8% QoQ.

This decline is driven by an unfavorable product mix for which NetApp management declines to provide further details. However, I believe this is driven by the current memory shortage, which affects flash storage that SSDs use, a required component of all flash arrays. While All Flash Array Q3 annualized revenue grew 11% YoY, I believe some enterprise customers are settling for lower-margin hybrid flash arrays and even all-HDD storage systems, amid the challenging economic conditions. But again, 55% gross margin is still a magnificent number for a hardware provider.

Operating profit for the quarter is $533 million, up 8% YoY, driven by operating leverage; in other words, NetApp delivered a higher revenue while maintaining its operating expense. This translates to a non-GAAP operating margin of 31.1% or a GAAP operating margin of 25.3%, which deducts stock compensation, amortization of intangible assets, and restructuring charges, and I believe is a more dependable number for an income statement.

Free cash flow is at $271 million. Capital returned to shareholders for the quarter is $303 million, of which $200 million is allocated to share repurchases and $103 million to dividends. This is the beauty of a capital-light business model like NetApp as most of the earnings can be returned to shareholders without derailing its competitive position.

Margin Stability And Expansion In Question Amid Memory Shortage

Whenever I read or listen to an earnings call, I always count the number of questions about a particular subject. This allows the investor to get a sense of which factor the market is focusing on to assess and determine the company's valuation. Of course, this is ambiguous as no one can precisely determine what the market is thinking. Regardless, looking at analysts' questions is often a good start, one I believe can be a source of good insights into any company's operations and fundamentals. During the Q3 2026 earnings call, roughly 60% of the analysts' questions(including follow-ups) are about margin while the rest are about NetApp's growth prospects; around 20% is on AI and the rest are on NetApp's other growth vectors like All Flash Array and Public Cloud. A significant portion of that 60% is about the continued memory shortage, implying the analysts' concern about the potential margin implication of this commodity shortage on NetApp's profitability. NetApp management responded that commodity costs are usually passed through to customers. While I don't doubt the company's ability to pass this commodity cost through its customers, what worries me is the potential implication of higher commodity cost on its higher-margin all-flash array systems, which may force enterprise customers to trade down to lower-margin products such as lower-performing all-flash array or hybrid storage.

Why is margin so important for NetApp? For low-growth and intangible-heavy companies like NetApp, margin stability and expansion are the main drivers of shareholder value. The real value of companies like NetApp lies in its stability and reliable cash flow, enabling them to provide a bond-like return to shareholders, which often warrants a higher valuation multiple. This stability is driven by the company's sticky business model and the favorable industry equilibrium. The idea is that since it has a bond-like return to shareholders, the market will put an earnings yield(inverse of P/E) on it, at least close to the risk-free government yield. However, the crucial difference is a business can grow its return unlike a bond.

Despite the current memory tailwind, I believe NetApp's differentiated business model enables it to pass this commodity cost through its customers and maintain margin during this shortage. This is the beauty of NetApp's business; it is akin to the classic business that buys commodity and sells a differentiated brand. While it may not be a perfect and consistent margin trend as not much management can do to hedge volatile commodity costs. I'm confident that the company's attractive long-term economics will remain intact. The often bane to any long-term favorable industry economics, a drastic industry alteration, isn't likely to affect NetApp's industry, even during this turbulent technological landscape, driven by AI. In fact, while growth won't be commensurate with the growth AI computing and networking currently enjoy, I believe there will be more demand for enterprise storage as AI continues to develop(More on this later)

While NetApp won't deliver a growth stock-like performance, pockets of NetApp's growth will come reliably from segments such as All-Flash Array and Public Cloud. The former can benefit from enterprise AI as it demands a performant, reliable storage solution. The latter can capture enterprise customers migrating towards a hybrid storage implementation. Furthermore, NetApp's public cloud division will bridge its customers' data located within their on-premises storage infrastructure with the AI Cloud, where most of the computing investments are made, thereby capturing a portion of the AI TAM growth. That said, the seemingly AI story that companies focus on computing and networking currently enjoys remains elusive for NetApp.

An AI Story That Remains Elusive But AI Inference May Change The Story

For the quarter, NetApp management disclosed that AI deals increased from 200 last quarter to 300. This is a 50% increase, which is promising. However, information regarding these deals remains vague and scant as management is refraining from providing useful information such as their total value relative to NetApp's total backlog. The best detail the management has disclosed among investors so far is the workload nature of these AI deals. According to NetApp management, 60% of these deals are for data preparation and data lakes, 20% for training and fine-tuning and 20% remaining for RAG and inference. Management has also disclosed that higher-margin all-flash arrays are used for performant use cases while hybrid flash arrays(storage that utilizes SSD and HDD) are used for use cases that require a mix of performance and capacity.

In traditional computing, data storage works in a complementary way with the CPU. Neither one can operate in isolation. Both must work together to deliver value to users. I believe this relationship won't change in AI computing. The general consensus among consulting firms such as McKinsey and Bain is that effective data management with proper guardrails is necessary to realize value from AI. NetApp, as one of the enterprise storage market leaders, can help enterprises with data management activities such as data preparation (60% of NetApp's 300 AI deals), to realize value from AI.

Given data's crucial role in AI, an investor should wonder why, despite the AI investment boom, this tailwind remains elusive for NetApp, whose revenue CAGR since 2022 has been a paltry 1.5%.

On a positive note, NetApp has managed to grow its profits at a higher rate than its revenue, a testament to its unrelenting efforts to maximize profitability.

But this is unsustainable and sooner or later will run its course because a business cannot cut its way to growth. So what's hindering NetApp from benefiting from the AI boom? A comment by NetApp CEO George Kurian from its Q2 2026 earnings.

Yes. I think we've always said the compute and network buildout would be both bigger and earlier than the storage buildout. I think it is because the training of large language models and the algorithms that are being used for AI does not require a lot of storage, right? Nobody is creating a second copy of all the internet data to train open AI, and so that buildout, of course, will be bigger and earlier than storage.

The data that trains AI models already exists on the internet, and training models doesn't involve creating more data; therefore, no demand for more storage capacity. However, this is poised to change as the industry shifts its attention from AI training to inference. AI will go from analyzing and understanding existing data to creating it. And where will these newly created data be stored? Of course, to the storage infrastructure that NetApp provides. That said, an investor should restrain its expectations, as clear challenges and hurdles remain that prevent these catalysts from materializing. The good thing is that NetApp is currently selling at an attractive price, a price where the vindication for an investment doesn't entirely rest on the premise of enterprise AI adoption.

Valuation

In Q3 2026 earnings, NetApp provided its full-year guidance.

For the year, the company expects EPS in the range of $7.92 to 8.02. At the midpoint of $7.97, this translates to a 12.3x P/E ratio or rougly 8% earnings yield; compared to the long-term risk-free rate of 5%, it seemingly offers an excellent value for a strong stock like NetApp. However, do note that this EPS guidance is non-GAAP and includes major line items such as stock-based compensation, which is charged to earnings under GAAP.

On the reconciliation of GAAP guidance to Non-GAAP, NetApp forecast its full-year EPS in the range of $6.07 to $6.17. With a midpoint EPS of around $6.12, this translates to roughly 16x P/E ratio or 6% earnings yield. When compared to the long-term risk-free rate of close to 4.8%, I believe this is still a good yield for a company that has demonstrated stable and robust earnings growth. Additionally, NetApp's earnings can still grow, increasing future yield for investors, unlike a bond, whose returns are fixed.

Moreover, for a heavily intangible-asset company that immediately incurs expenses in R&D and marketing, which nowadays drive more long-term economic benefits than tangible fixed assets, I believe this is a good yield. If we capitalize these expenses the same way we do with tangible assets, reported earnings should be higher. Lastly, I believe that NetApp will benefit from the continued momentum of AI inference. Therefore, I will upgrade NetApp to 'Strong Buy.'

Risk To Investment In NetApp

Risk to the Investment thesis is the current hurdles facing enterprise AI adoption, which is a prerequisite to the AI inference catalyst I articulated earlier. Enterprises currently face challenges and hurdles in integrating AI. As mentioned earlier, according to MIT, 95% of AI projects failed to meet their respective performance target. I believe AI's nascent stage is attributable to this statistic.
